story:
Breakfast was impossible. The bread was hard, the sun too hot, my lips cracked, my stomach knotted. The unease had built for weeks. At first I called it seasickness, but we had long since reached shore. I knew the true cause but had refused to name it. Fear is un-Roman. Yet it had grown too large to ignore.

I sat on a dune and tried to see as a Roman ought. So many ships. Such might. So many men. No city could withstand such power. We are the legion, the dread of nations. At the mere sight of us the enemy would flee. If not, the Gods would judge. Our cause is just, I told myself. I have been a good Roman. The Gods will not let me perish. I will live beyond this day. For a moment, those thoughts steadied me.

Then the questions—grotesque and sharp—turned in my gut. What of the enemy? Are their lives worth less than mine? Do they deserve to die? Who am I to decide? We have lost to them before; why not again? Would the Gods spare me, or make me a martyr? I looked around. Many faces were ashen, eyes bruised with sleeplessness. Young Plautus, hardly fifteen, was green and gazing at the sand. A priest of Mars offered prayer; Plautus turned away. He entered his tent, then emerged with shield, sword, and helmet glinting in the African sun. He was still afraid, but he had accepted whatever the Fates would spin. I envied him.

My eyes went to Flavius, our commander, loudest of us all. A veteran of the Spanish campaigns, he bore scars from many battles with Celtic tribes, but this would be his first time facing Carthaginian steel. His grandfather had fallen at Cannae, his father at Zama—both crushed by Hannibal's genius before Flavius was even born. The stories of their deaths, told by his weeping mother, had shaped his entire life. If any man had cause to seek vengeance against this city that had spawned Rome's greatest enemy, it was he. But not me. I did not know these people or their lands. They had never wronged me. My little farm in Mediolanum lay far away, untouched. Still, our leaders insisted Carthage threatened Rome itself and demanded preemptive defense. Perhaps war was needed. Perhaps not. Both choices seemed senseless; both filled me with dread.

As we marched on the city, armor baking, I could not stop thinking of how men would soon try to kill me: arrows, oil, the thrust of spear, the slash of steel. And I could not blame them. They defended their homes. In the very moment I needed Roman courage, I could not understand our own. This is not our land. This grass is not for our livestock. Can this be righteous? I did not want this. Yet I marched. To turn back meant execution as a traitor; to go forward meant kill or be killed. I thought Romans above such barbarous choices.

The walls of Carthage rose, cruel and high. The siege towers, lurching over rough ground, swayed as if seasick themselves. Above, a black bird flew with a serpent in its beak—an omen the augurs would call either good or ill. The enemy's chanting rolled down, strong and strange, almost pleasant. Perhaps they were not barbarians at all.

We did not break stride. I was about to meet death or deal it, and I had not decided which. The first volley came, the air between release and impact a terrible silence. We drew breath, raised our shields, and listened to the arrows thud into wood. The battle had begun, and still I had not made my decision.
